title: EchoGuard: Real-Time-Capable Full-Duplex Audio Front-End for Voice Agents
subtitle: Echo suppression • Barge-in detection • ASR robustness • SDK-style Python package
description: A real-time-capable speech front-end for voice-agent systems that suppresses far-end speaker echo, stabilizes barge-in detection, and improves ASR robustness under full-duplex echo/noise conditions.
longDescription: Built EchoGuard as a reusable Python SDK-style full-duplex speech front-end for voice-agent pipelines. The system uses the far-end reference signal to estimate acoustic echo delay with GCC-PHAT, align the reference, learn an FIR echo-path filter from far-end-dominant audio, suppress the estimated speaker echo, and run an echo-aware residual barge-in detector. Evaluation is performed through file-based streaming simulation with 20 ms frames and 10 ms hop timing, reporting real-time factor, echo reduction, SNR gain, barge-in reliability, and downstream ASR WER. On the main full-duplex scenario, EchoGuard achieved 0.0 ms echo-delay error, 18.56 dB echo reduction, +7.30 dB SNR gain, and 0.059 real-time factor. Compared with a raw microphone energy gate, false barge-in rate dropped from 0.411 to 0.000. Across 10 Mini LibriSpeech full-duplex pairs, the same ASR model saw mean WER improve from 0.747 on raw microphone audio to 0.115 after EchoGuard front-end processing.
metrics: Main scenario: 0.0 ms echo-delay error, 18.56 dB echo reduction, +7.30 dB SNR gain
Runtime: 0.059 real-time factor in file-based streaming evaluation
Barge-in: false trigger rate reduced from 0.411 to 0.000
ASR: mean WER reduced from 0.747 to 0.115 over 10 Mini LibriSpeech full-duplex pairs
highlights: Built a reusable Python SDK-style speech front-end for full-duplex voice-agent audio.
Used GCC-PHAT to estimate acoustic echo delay and align far-end reference audio with microphone echo.
Learned an FIR echo-path filter from far-end-dominant audio to avoid corrupting the echo model during double-talk.
Implemented echo suppression and residual filtering to reduce far-end speaker leakage before downstream ASR.
Designed an echo-aware barge-in detector using residual energy, learned noise floor, far-end activity, correlation checks, and persistence logic.
Achieved 0.0 ms echo-delay error, 18.56 dB echo reduction, +7.30 dB SNR gain, and 0.059 real-time factor on the main scenario.
Reduced false barge-in rate from 0.411 for a raw microphone energy gate to 0.000 with EchoGuard, with 40 ms detection latency.
Evaluated downstream ASR impact over 10 Mini LibriSpeech full-duplex pairs, reducing mean WER from 0.747 to 0.115.
Generated waveform, ERLE, barge-in comparison, baseline-energy, ASR spectrogram, JSON, and CSV artifacts for reproducible reporting.
Packaged the core logic as an editable Python module with `pyproject.toml` and clean imports through `from echoguard import EchoGuardPipeline`.

title: VCM-Lite Edge Camera: Task-Aware Semantic Video Compression
subtitle: WebRTC ingest • Arduino Uno Q • ISP-Lite • Semantic ROI compression • Adaptive controller
description: A VCM-inspired semantic edge-camera pipeline that streams traffic video over WebRTC to Arduino Uno Q, preserves vehicle ROIs through task-aware context+ROI compression, and uses a closed-loop adaptive controller to balance bitrate, latency, and detection quality.
longDescription: Built VCM-Lite as a real-time-capable semantic edge-camera system for traffic monitoring on Arduino Uno Q. The pipeline streams video through a WebRTC ingest path, bridges decoded frames into a C++ engine, applies ISP-Lite enhancement, selects task-relevant vehicle regions using a YOLOv8n-style detector fine-tuned from a pretrained model for traffic classes, compresses each frame as a low-resolution context image plus higher-quality object ROI tiles, reconstructs a semantic frame, and validates whether vehicle evidence is preserved after compression. The main contribution is a closed-loop adaptive semantic controller that observes FPS, latency, queue pressure, ROI area, semantic bitrate, task preservation loss, and detector confidence drop, then adjusts ROI quality, context quality, ROI budget, detector interval, and re-encoding behavior. On dense traffic, the system achieved 138.92 ms average latency, 316.09 ms P95 latency, 693.89 kbps semantic bitrate, 0.0247 task preservation loss, 24.82 dB semantic PSNR, and 0.9748 SSIM while preserving the highest ROI area ratio among the test scenarios. The project includes FastAPI dashboard observability, runtime JSONL logs, benchmark scripts, generated comparison plots, and MCU-side health telemetry for edge-device monitoring.
metrics: Dense traffic: 138.92 ms average latency, 316.09 ms P95 latency, 693.89 kbps semantic bitrate
Task preservation: 0.0247 task loss on dense traffic with 0.9748 SSIM and 24.82 dB PSNR
ISP-Lite: dense traffic brightness improved from 79.43 to 109.74 and contrast from 43.27 to 59.21
Scenario evaluation: sparse/object-light, dense traffic, and short low-light night stress clips with final CSV and plot reports
highlights: Built an end-to-end semantic edge-camera pipeline that streams video over WebRTC into Arduino Uno Q and processes frames in a C++ engine.
Implemented ISP-Lite preprocessing to improve brightness and contrast before ROI selection and semantic encoding.
Fine-tuned a pretrained YOLOv8n-style detector for traffic classes including car, van, bus, and others, then exported it to ONNX for edge inference.
Designed a VCM-inspired context+ROI compression format that keeps low-resolution scene context while preserving task-critical vehicle ROI tiles at higher quality.
Implemented semantic reconstruction that restores selected object ROIs into the context frame for task-focused machine interpretation rather than pixel-perfect playback.
Added task-preservation validation using reference detector confidence, reconstructed detector confidence, confidence drop, PSNR, SSIM, and task preservation loss.
Built a closed-loop adaptive controller that changes ROI quality, context quality, ROI limits, detector interval, and re-encoding based on latency, bitrate, ROI area, queue pressure, and task loss.
Achieved the strongest result on dense traffic: 138.92 ms average latency, 693.89 kbps semantic bitrate, 0.0247 task preservation loss, and 0.9748 SSIM.
Created a FastAPI dashboard showing WebRTC ingest status, semantic packet details, adaptive controller state, ISP metrics, task detector output, and reconstruction validation.
Added MCU-side telemetry to demonstrate a realistic edge split where the Linux side handles video processing and the internal MCU side reports compact device health.

Generated reproducible benchmark artifacts including JSONL logs, final comparison CSV, runtime breakdowns, and plots for FPS, latency, bitrate, ROI area, task loss, PSNR, SSIM, brightness, contrast, and ISP runtime.

Designed the architecture to be transferable beyond traffic monitoring by replacing the task detector and ROI policy for factory inspection, security cameras, warehouse robotics, retail monitoring, or wildlife sensing.

title: VIDEX: Intelligent Video Exploration & Summarization
subtitle: Serverless ingest • Autoencoder compression • RAG + LLM answers
description: Cost-aware pipeline that ingests CCTV-style videos to S3, compresses with a CNN autoencoder, generates metadata, and answers natural-language queries by surfacing clips with timestamps.
longDescription: Secure ingest via API Gateway + Lambda into S3 (public access blocked). Practical compression using a 2D CNN autoencoder (train MSE≈0.0045, val≈0.0043) and S3 lifecycle to Glacier Deep Archive. Per-video metadata feeds MiniLM embeddings and vector search; FAISS outperformed Chroma on this dataset (index ~1.16s vs 1.75s; query ~30ms vs 62ms). An LLM synthesizes concise answers with file names and timestamps. Production path: FFmpeg in a Lambda container or ECS Fargate, with S3→SQS→Lambda/Step Functions for scale.
highlights: Secure ingest: API Gateway + Lambda → S3 (block public access, least-privilege IAM).
Compression: 2D CNN autoencoder; practical run local; MSE≈0.0045 train / 0.0043 val.
Storage policy: originals→Glacier Deep Archive; compressed kept hot for playback.
RAG: MiniLM embeddings; FAISS vs Chroma (index 1.16s vs 1.75s; query 30ms vs 62ms).
LLM answers with video names and timestamps; avoids hallucination via grounded context.
Scale path: FFmpeg in Lambda container or ECS Fargate; S3→SQS→Lambda; Step Functions.

title: Head-Test QC: AI-Assisted Acoustic Quality Control for Headsets
subtitle: DSP measurements • Calibrated defect screening • Interpretable triage
description: DSP-first acoustic QC system for headsets that combines ESS-based measurements, deterministic guardrails, and calibrated ML screening to detect subtle defects, improve accepted-case decision quality, and explain failures with feature-level attributions.
longDescription: Built an end-to-end headset QC pipeline that measures latency, frequency-response mismatch, channel imbalance, leakage-related artifacts, and distortion proxies from ESS-based impulse-response analysis. The system uses sequential L→gap→R acquisition, beamforming-assisted cleanup, and inverse deconvolution to extract robust acoustic features before applying rule-based QC guardrails and a calibrated ML screening layer. Compared with deterministic guardrails alone, the ML layer substantially improved binary PASS/DEFECT screening quality and more than doubled defect-triage balanced accuracy. The final system supports transparent pass/fail decisions, review-aware screening through abstention, per-unit severity estimation, and SHAP-based explanation of defect-risk predictions.
highlights: Designed a DSP-first QC pipeline using Farina ESS sweeps, sequential L→gap→R acquisition, beamforming-assisted cleanup, and inverse deconvolution for channel-isolated impulse-response measurement.
Extracted interpretable headset QC features including latency mismatch, spectral tilt, band-wise frequency-response mismatch, flatness, leakage proxy, late-energy ratio, and channel asymmetry.
Built a calibrated binary defect screener that achieved PR-AUC = 0.762 and ROC-AUC = 0.697 on simulated headset defect data.
Improved accepted-case balanced accuracy to 0.692 at 0.828 coverage using an abstention-aware decision policy for safer QC screening.
Outperformed deterministic guardrails on binary PASS/DEFECT screening by +0.166 balanced accuracy.
Improved defect triage balanced accuracy from 0.209 (rule-based baseline) to 0.541 with ML-assisted classification across DELAY, EQ_TILT, XTALK, and COMPLEX cases.
Added per-unit severity regression for delay, tilt, and crosstalk-related defect estimates to support technician-facing diagnosis.
Integrated SHAP-based explanation on the binary screening model to surface the dominant DSP features driving each defect decision.
Generated benchmark plots and confusion matrices for guardrails-vs-ML uplift, ROC/PR screening performance, coverage-vs-quality tradeoff, and defect triage behavior.

title: Network AI Agent: Self-Improving Wireless Link Optimization
subtitle: LSTM state classification • Self-RAG retrieval • LangGraph agent loop
description: Agentic control loop for wireless links: classifies link quality via LSTM, retrieves similar past cases with FAISS, and executes optimized actions with simulated or real radio controls—improving from experience.
longDescription: End-to-end agent for adaptive wireless link management. Ingests streaming 15×7 windows of QoS metrics (SNR, BER, loss, jitter, throughput, trends) scaled consistently across the pipeline. LSTM classifier labels states (Good/Moderate/Poor) with ~98% accuracy, forming the perception front-end. Good states short-circuit; Moderate/Poor trigger a LangGraph loop: retrieve similar past cases from a FAISS-based Self-RAG memory, decide on an action via heuristics or budget-aware LLM consultation, execute via simulated feature deltas or real radio commands, re-evaluate, and store outcomes. Actions are normalized to 5 canonical types for determinism. Success is judged by label flips, probability gains, and QoS improvements. System logs full traces and metrics (success rate, probability gain, memory hit/win rate, LLM usage) and improves as Self-RAG grows. Designed for seamless simulation-to-hardware transition with safety guardrails.
highlights: Streaming input: 15×7 windows of QoS metrics with state-aware synthetic generation.
Consistent scaling across classification, retrieval, and simulation to avoid OOD errors.
Perception: LSTM sequence model with fixed class order, ~98% test accuracy.
Decision layer: Self-RAG with FAISS retrieval + similarity scoring; budget-aware LLM calls.
Canonical action set: QPSK+FEC, Increase Tx Power, Band Switch, Beamforming, Relay Node.
Simulation executor: state- and time-ramped feature deltas for realistic link changes.
Evaluation: triple-criteria success (label, probability, QoS deltas).
Memory updates after each step; failures stored for policy refinement.
LangGraph state machine: classify → retrieve → act → evaluate → learn.
Deployable to hardware with real radio controls and safety guardrails.

title: Federated Acoustic Anomaly Detection for Industrial Pumps
subtitle: MFCC feature extraction • AE/VAE anomaly detection • Federated Learning • XAI • RAG
description: Privacy-preserving anomaly detection pipeline with per-client explainability and a local GenAI assistant—trained across sites without sharing raw audio.
longDescription: Built a privacy-preserving acoustic anomaly detection system for industrial pumps using MFCC features and unsupervised models (Autoencoder and Variational Autoencoder). Simulated multi-site deployment via Flower/FedAvg, training models locally at each client without moving data. Implemented per-client evaluation and explainability (SHAP-on-PCA heatmaps mapped to MFCC×time bins) to pinpoint anomalous frequency bands and frames. Added a RAG-powered GenAI assistant that answers technician questions from local artifacts and guides troubleshooting without data leakage. Benchmarked AE vs VAE globally and per client (AUC, recall@2σ, F1), monitored federated training round metrics, and delivered local evaluation reports, XAI outputs, and per-client vector stores for natural-language queries.
highlights: MFCC extraction (~313×15 → 4695-D) with z-score scaling per client.
Unsupervised models: AE (MSE loss) and VAE (MSE + β·KL) with ~0.76–0.87 accuracy and up to ~0.90 AUC in FL runs.
Federated training via Flower/FedAvg with 3 simulated clients and per-round centralized evaluation.
Per-client evaluation: local thresholding, confusion matrix, ROC/AUC, classification reports.
Explainability: SHAP with PCA bridge to produce interpretable MFCC×time heatmaps.
Local GenAI assistant: FAISS vector store + HF MiniLM embeddings for private NL queries.
Privacy preserved—no raw audio leaves client; only model updates are shared.
Benchmarked AE vs VAE performance under heterogeneity; noted recall trade-offs.
Engineering notes on class imbalance handling, activation alignment, and personalization.
Portable architecture for industrial edge devices with offline RAG search.

title: Adaptive ISAC-MIMO for Multi-Human Tracking
subtitle: 64×MIMO • Convex power control • MUSIC + Kalman tracking
description: Four-phase ISAC beamforming system for a 64-antenna 2.4 GHz base station in disaster zones, balancing comms SINR with survivor detection via Doppler, super-resolution AoA, and real-time beam steering.
longDescription: Base station with 64 co-phased antennas (≤λ/2 spacing) forms up to 8 simultaneous beams under Monte-Carlo-verified SINR limits. A fixed communication beam (90°) ensures ≥10 SNR while 7 sensing beams sweep 360° in 5 s to detect motion via Doppler. Convex optimisation reallocates power based on motion rates. STFT Doppler spectrograms identify gait/breathing patterns; MUSIC AoA and Kalman filtering predict future target positions for millisecond-scale steering. System sustains robust detection and low-latency tracking in 40-path Rayleigh fading with 1 W EIRP.
highlights: 64-element 2.4 GHz MIMO array; fully overlapped digital beams for comms + sensing.
Monte-Carlo beam limit search yields safe max of 8 beams without SINR loss.
5 s azimuth sweep logs Doppler-positive angles; noisy detections get lower power.
Convex optimiser allocates beam power by Doppler-derived activity weights.
Micro-Doppler analysis extracts gait/breathing signatures; peak counting estimates people.
MUSIC AoA + Kalman prediction delivers smooth, real-time beam steering.

title: Convex Relaxations for Rigid & Non-Rigid Registration
subtitle: SDP for rigid alignment • ADMM for non-rigid deformations • 3D vision
description: Explored convex optimization approaches to image registration, applying Semidefinite Programming (SDP) to rigid alignment and ADMM to non-rigid deformation for robust, globally optimal transformations in 3D vision tasks.
longDescription: Rigid and non-rigid registration are central to aligning datasets in medical imaging, robotics, and AR. This work reformulates both within a convex optimization framework: rigid alignment via Semidefinite Programming (SDP) to overcome SO(3) non-convexity and initialization sensitivity; non-rigid alignment via Alternating Direction Method of Multipliers (ADMM) to handle complex deformations while maintaining stability. Benchmarked against ICP-based methods, the convex relaxations improved robustness to local minima and convergence speed. Implementation included point cloud preprocessing, transformation estimation, and evaluation on synthetic and real-world datasets.
highlights: Rigid registration via SDP for globally optimal rotation and translation estimation.
Non-rigid registration via ADMM for stable deformation modeling.
Overcomes ICP limitations: faster convergence and reduced sensitivity to initialization.
Applied to synthetic & real datasets in medical imaging and AR contexts.
Demonstrated robustness against local minima in both rigid and non-rigid cases.

title: Rate–Distortion Optimization in Modern Image Codecs
subtitle: JPEG vs JPEG XL vs AVIF • PSNR/SSIM & BD-Rate • MOS study
description: Comparative RDO study of JPEG, JPEG XL, and AVIF using Kodak images at matched bitrates (0.1/0.3/0.5/0.7 BPP). Evaluates objective quality (PSNR, SSIM, BD-Rate/BD-PSNR), subjective MOS, and artifact patterns; shows why AVIF/JPEG XL outperform JPEG—especially at low bitrates.
longDescription: Using the Kodak dataset (24 images, 768×512), we encoded each image with JPEG (MATLAB imwrite), JPEG XL (cjxl), and AVIF (avifenc) at 0.1/0.3/0.5/0.7 BPP and decoded to PNG for fair analysis. Objective curves (Bitrate–PSNR/SSIM) plus BD-Rate/BD-PSNR show clear efficiency gains over JPEG: JPEG XL saves ~28.5% bitrate at equal PSNR (BD-Rate 28.47%; +2.88 dB BD-PSNR) and AVIF saves ~57% (BD-Rate 57.04%; +4.42 dB BD-PSNR). A 10-rater MOS (Single Stimulus, ITU-R BT.500) aligns with the metrics: AVIF ≳ JPEG XL ≫ JPEG, with AVIF best at low BPP thanks to perceptual quantization and CABAC. Visual inspections confirm fewer blocking/ringing and better texture retention for AVIF/JPEG XL. We also compare RDO building blocks: quantization (JPEG fixed; JPEG XL adaptive; AVIF perceptual), entropy coding (JPEG RLE+Huffman; JPEG XL ANS; AVIF CABAC), and transforms (JPEG 8×8 DCT; JPEG XL VarDCT 8–32; AVIF hybrid DCT/DWT + variable blocks).
highlights: Dataset & protocol: Kodak suite; encode at 0.1/0.3/0.5/0.7 BPP; decode to PNG for apples-to-apples analysis.
Objective gains: JPEG XL vs JPEG — BD-Rate 28.47% lower; +2.88 dB BD-PSNR.
Objective gains: AVIF vs JPEG — BD-Rate 57.04% lower; +4.42 dB BD-PSNR.
Subjective study: 10 raters (BT.500 Single Stimulus); AVIF best across bitrates; JPEG lags at low BPP.
Artifacts: AVIF/JPEG XL reduce blocking/banding/ringing and preserve textures better than JPEG at low BPP.
Quantization: JPEG fixed; JPEG XL adaptive; AVIF perceptual/HVS-guided.
Entropy coding: JPEG RLE+Huffman; JPEG XL ANS; AVIF CABAC for low-bitrate efficiency.
Transforms: JPEG 8×8 DCT; JPEG XL VarDCT (8–32); AVIF hybrid DCT/DWT with variable block sizes.
Tooling: MATLAB imwrite (JPEG), cjxl (JPEG XL), avifenc (AVIF); matching bitrates via binary/quality search.
Takeaway: Modern RDO (adaptive/perceptual + stronger entropy/transform choices) yields superior quality at lower bitrates.

title: Reconstruction Quality of Gaussian, Max-Pooling & Max-Averaging Pyramids
subtitle: SSIM-based evaluation • Optimal σ & down/up factors • Speed vs quality trade-offs
description: One-level image pyramids compared for reconstruction quality and efficiency. Tuned parameters (σ, interpolation, down/up factors), measured SSIM, and benchmarked time/memory to reveal when each method wins.
longDescription: Built and analyzed 1-level Gaussian, Max-Pooling, and Max-Averaging pyramids. Using frequency-domain analysis (FFT + radial spectra) we identified σ≈1.5 as the optimal Gaussian bandwidth. Across methods, bicubic interpolation delivered the highest SSIM, and a ×2 down/upsampling factor gave the best quality–compression balance. Computational profiling showed Gaussian Pyramid is fastest/most memory-efficient, while Max-Averaging yields the best reconstruction SSIM. The study consolidates method choices (σ, interpolation, factors) and quantifies the quality–efficiency trade-offs for multirate image processing.
highlights: Optimal Gaussian bandwidth: σ ≈ 1.5 from FFT & radial frequency analysis.
Bicubic interpolation consistently achieved the highest SSIM across methods.
Down/upsampling factor of 2 delivered the best reconstruction quality.
Quality vs speed: Max-Averaging best SSIM; Gaussian Pyramid fastest/most memory-efficient.
Reported concrete timings/memory (example config): Gaussian down 0.0023s / up 0.0028s / ~11.42MB; Max-Pooling down 0.0271s / up 0.0009s / ~11.45MB; Max-Averaging down 0.0628s / up 0.0008s / ~11.64MB.
Evaluation images: Lena & UCSB (grayscale) for consistent SSIM comparisons.

title: Fire Vital: Real-Time Firefighter Health Monitoring & Command Center Collaboration
subtitle: MAX30102 vitals • LoRaWAN + TTN • MQTT web dashboards • Piezo energy harvesting
description: End-to-end system that senses SpO₂, heart rate, and temperature on-person, streams via LoRaWAN to The Things Network, and visualizes live trends in a secured web UI using MQTT—powered by a piezoelectric harvester for low-power field use.
longDescription: Wearable node (Arduino Uno + MAX30102) acquires vitals and transmits over LoRa (shield + gateway) to TTN. Using TTN’s MQTT integration, a password-protected website subscribes to live payloads and renders time-stamped graphs for incident commanders. A piezoelectric generator harvests mechanical energy to supplement power. Field tests measured HR/SpO₂/temperature trends for five users, end-to-end latency of ~8–10 s from node to cloud, and a working range up to ≥2 km outdoors (shorter indoors), with LOS projections up to ~10–15 km. The report documents block diagrams, sensor/I²C bring-up, TTN console traces, MQTT subscriptions, and UI pages (home/about/login/visualization).
highlights: Sensing: MAX30102 measures heart rate, SpO₂, and temperature; I²C scanner verifies bus address.
Connectivity: LoRa shield → gateway → TTN; MQTT broker integration fans out live data to subscribers.
UI: Password-protected website renders real-time graphs of HR/SpO₂/temperature with timestamps.
Energy: Piezoelectric generator harvests mechanical stress to supplement power for the node.
Latency: End-to-end cloud arrival typically ~8–10 s (node → gateway → TTN → subscriber).
Range: Outdoor links ≥2 km in tests; shorter indoors; LOS projections ~10 km (urban) to ~15 km (rural).
Observed vitals (5 users): HR ≈ 65–83 BPM, SpO₂ ≈ 94–98%, Temperature ≈ 33–37 °C.
Artifacts: TTN console payloads, MQTT subscription logs, and web UI pages (home/about/login/plots) captured.
Comparative note: Versus prior work, Fire Vital emphasizes low-power operation and simple web-based situational awareness.

title: Hydro Watch: Real-Time Water Quality Analytics & Filtration
subtitle: LoRaWAN sensing • XGBoost potability • MQTT dashboards • Auto filtration
description: End-to-end IoT system that senses pH/TDS/turbidity/temperature, streams via LoRaWAN to TTN, visualizes live metrics over MQTT, predicts potability with XGBoost, and triggers a multi-stage filter automatically.
longDescription: Built a field-ready water-quality pipeline: Arduino-class end node (pH, TDS, turbidity, DS18B20) → LoRa shield → gateway → The Things Network (TTN). A password-protected web app subscribes to TTN’s MQTT to render live gauges/plots and show potability. Five ML models were evaluated; XGBoost was integrated for online potability prediction. An alert path (WhatsApp) notifies on non-potable readings; an ESP32 listens via WebSocket and drives a pump through a layered filter (gravel, fine sand, activated charcoal, CaCO₃, zeolite, kaolin, cotton). Prototype achieved ~8–10 s cloud latency, outdoor range ≥2 km (LOS projections ~10–15 km), and a reported XGBoost accuracy of ~99.9% on the project dataset. Solar panels can power the node for low-infrastructure deployments.
highlights: Sensors: pH, TDS, turbidity, DS18B20 temperature on Arduino-class end node.
Connectivity: LoRa shield → gateway → TTN; web UI subscribes via MQTT with login protection.
Live visualization: real-time meter gauges and trend plots; potability page auto-refresh.
ML: evaluated 5 models; XGBoost selected (reported ~99.9% accuracy on dataset) for potability.
Alerts: non-potable → WhatsApp notification; WebSocket to ESP32 triggers 2-minute filtration.
Filter stack: gravel → fine sand → activated charcoal → CaCO₃ → zeolite → kaolin → cotton.
Latency & range: ~8–10 s end-to-end; observed outdoor range ≥2 km; LOS projections ~10–15 km.
Ops details: I²C scanner for sensor bring-up; TTN MQTT integration; secured local web server.
Energy: solar panels used to power sensors/microcontroller in prototype.
Artifacts: TTN payload logs, MQTT traces, website pages (home/about/login/visualization), range table and model performance table.

title: Advanced Crime Classification with Deep Learning
subtitle: Multimodal CNN/RNN • Real-time monitoring • Hotspot mapping & APIs
description: End-to-end, multimodal system that classifies crimes from text, images/video, and audio; streams alerts in real time; and maps hotspots using geolocation APIs for proactive public-safety analytics.
longDescription: Built a scalable pipeline that ingests CCTV frames/video, police-report text, and audio snippets, standardizes them (frame extraction, text normalization, noise reduction), and classifies incidents using deep learning (CNNs for imagery/video frames, RNN/Transformer-based models for text, optional audio embeddings). A streaming service performs real-time inference and pushes alerts to a dashboard. Geolocation metadata is indexed to render live hotspot maps and time-of-day trends. The platform exposes a REST/Graph API for integration with existing systems, and supports cloud deployment (AWS/GCP) for horizontal scale. Emphasis is placed on privacy/ethics (PII scrubbing, access controls), explainability (feature/attention visualizations), and robust evaluation (precision/recall/F1 by class, latency budgets).
highlights: Multimodal ingest: text (reports), images/video (CCTV), and audio; unified preprocessing.
Models: CNNs for frames/video; RNN/Transformer for text; optional audio embeddings.
Real-time pipeline: streaming inference with alerting and latency budgets for live use.
Hotspot mapping: geolocation integration to visualize risk areas and temporal patterns.
Predictive analytics: trend detection for proactive resource planning.
APIs: REST/Graph endpoints for integrations; role-based access to outputs.
Cloud-native: deployable on AWS/GCP with autoscaling and message queues.
Evaluation: per-class precision/recall/F1, ROC/PR curves, confusion matrices.
Explainability: attention/grad-CAM style visuals and textual rationales for decisions.
Governance: PII scrubbing, audit logs, and policy guardrails for ethical compliance.

title: JitterTuner: Adaptive Jitter-Resilient DSP Pipeline (Software-Only)
subtitle: FreeRTOS tasks • Q15 FIR low-pass • Adaptive jitter buffer • Real-time metrics
description: Embedded-style DSP project that runs entirely in software: a bursty producer feeds a ring buffer; a FreeRTOS DSP task applies a Q15 FIR low-pass; and a tuner keeps latency low and underruns at zero under CPU/arrival jitter.
longDescription: JitterTuner models a real firmware audio/comm pipeline using FreeRTOS (Windows/MingW port). Data flows in 256-sample frames at 48 kHz through an O(1) ring buffer (capacity 8). A Producer task synthesizes a test signal (1 kHz passband + 10 kHz stopband + noise) with bursty arrivals; a DSP task consumes frames, injects CPU-time jitter, applies a 63-tap Q15 FIR low-pass (Hamming-windowed, unity-gain normalized), and measures spectral amplitudes via running sine/cosine projections. Two lightweight controllers stabilize the system: (1) a prefill tuner that sets the startup cushion (frames to accumulate before playback) using windowed statistics, and (2) a dynamic occupancy tuner that pops an extra frame when the buffer runs high or skips a slice when it runs low, keeping latency near a target. The project instruments pushes/pops/drops/underruns, occupancy (avg and percentiles), CPU time per frame, deadline overruns, and frequency response deltas—yielding a complete, reproducible demonstration of fixed-point DSP, RTOS tasking, and adaptive jitter control without any hardware.
highlights: Architecture: Producer → RingBuffer (CAP=8) → DSP (Q15 FIR) with FreeRTOS tasks and a mutex for shared state.
Filter: 63-tap Hamming low-pass, designed in float then quantized to Q15; linear-phase and unity DC gain.
Controllers: prefill tuner (windowed stats) + dynamic occupancy tuner (extra pop / throttle) to balance latency vs. safety.
Timing model: 256-sample frames at 48 kHz (~5.33 ms); DSP task runs at 5 ms with injected CPU jitter to stress deadlines.
Spectral verification: running single-bin projections at 1 kHz and 10 kHz to report passband loss and stopband attenuation.
Observability: pushes/pops/drops/underruns, occupancy histogram and P50/P95/P99, CPU time per frame, deadline-miss histogram.
Result (this run): 441 pushes, 433 pops, 0 drops, 0 underruns; prefill adapted 3→4→3→2→1 as conditions stabilized.
Latency: avg occupancy 5.56 frames → ~29.64 ms average latency; percentile occupancy P50/P95/P99 = 6 frames (~32 ms).
Dynamic actions: 36 extra pops and 2 throttles held the buffer near target (TARGET_OCC=4) without glitches.
Frequency response: 1 kHz passband loss ≈ −0.17 dB; 10 kHz stopband attenuation ≈ −66.9 dB (fixed-point runtime).
CPU budget: ~105 µs average (≈2% of 5.33 ms) and ~523 µs max (≈9.8%); 0 deadline overruns.
Software-only: no hardware required; portable to MCU targets by swapping the FreeRTOS port and I/O layer.

title: URLLC-in-the-Loop — TDMA + FEC
subtitle: TDMA scheduling • k-of-n erasure coding • Dual-path diversity • GE burst model • SLO sweep
description: A software-only URLLC stack for industrial STOP decisions that co-designs deterministic scheduling (TDMA), k-of-n FEC, and optional dual-path diversity to hit ≥99.9% on-time with P99 ≤ 20 ms at minimal bandwidth overhead.
longDescription: This project models a real-time control link where an endpoint detector must deliver tiny STOP messages within strict deadlines. A 20 Hz producer emits decisions; a scheduler sends either best-effort (subject to heavy-tailed queueing jitter) or via TDMA micro-slots (nearly deterministic). The channel adds delay/loss using either an i.i.d. model or a Gilbert–Elliott (GE) burst model; a k-of-n erasure decoder reconstructs when any k of n chunks arrive; and an optional dual-path mode splits chunks across two independent links. The framework sweeps scheduler mode, path count, and (n,k) to automatically choose the lowest-overhead design meeting SLOs (≥99.9% on-time, P99 ≤ 20 ms), and includes an end-to-end demo where a lightweight DSP endpoint detector (two-window mean + hysteresis on synthetic 1 kHz traces) produces decisions so results map to real control behavior. Artifacts include CSV metrics, P99-vs-overhead plots, and a concise summary of the recommended configuration.
highlights: Architecture: Producer (20 Hz) → FEC(k-of-n) → Scheduler (best-effort or TDMA) → Channel(s) (i.i.d. or GE bursts; 1–2 paths) → k-of-n Decoder → Metrics.
SLOs: optimize for ≥99.9% on-time delivery with P99 latency ≤ 20 ms while minimizing bandwidth overhead (≈ n/k).
Baseline (IID, best-effort, n=1,k=1): ~98.17% on-time; P99 ≈ 18.24 ms over 1,200 msgs (60 s at 20 Hz).
TDMA (no FEC): tail collapse with P99 ≈ 5.12 ms (vs 18.24 ms best-effort) under the same IID channel.
FEC on IID: TDMA (n=3,k=1) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 4.66 ms (overhead 3.0×); TDMA (n=4,k=2) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 5.57 ms (overhead 2.0×).
GE burst model + diversity: TDMA (paths=1, n=3,k=1) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 4.70 ms; TDMA (paths=2, n=3,k=1) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 4.71 ms; TDMA (paths=2, n=4,k=2) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 5.83 ms.
SLO-constrained sweep: 20 feasible configs found; recommended minimal-overhead design = TDMA, 1 path, (n=5,k=3) → success=1.000, P99 ≈ 6.89 ms at only 1.67× overhead.
Process impact proxy: reports late-rate and excess-lateness averages; plots P99-vs-overhead and overshoot-reduction vs overhead.
End-to-end demo (DSP detector + network, 50 ms budget): baseline net ≈ 90.0% E2E success (net P99 ≈ 17.9 ms); robust net (TDMA, 2 paths, n=4,k=2) ≈ 93.1% E2E success (net P99 ≈ 5.92 ms).
Knobs: toughen bursts (GE loss_bad ↑, p_bg ↓), enable dual-path, and tune (n,k) to trade overhead vs reliability; speed up detector via smaller frame/hysteresis/threshold.

Outputs: CSVs for each experiment, summary.json, and PNG plots (P99 vs overhead, overshoot reduction). Run `python run_all.py` for the full pipeline or `python quick_start.py` for a compact sweep + demo.
Resume-ready impact: met ≥99.9% on-time with ≤6.9 ms P99 at ≤1.67× overhead; SLO-driven design search; validated end-to-end with a working DSP producer.

title: Predictive Adaptive RS-FEC Controller for Multi-Lane PAM4 Links
subtitle: One-step-ahead lane-health prediction • Fixed-budget parity control • CTLE/FFE equalization • RS-FEC reliability optimization
description: A telemetry-driven predictive control framework for 4-lane PAM4 links that uses DSP/equalization metrics and one-step-ahead lane-health prediction to proactively reallocate RS-FEC parity under a fixed total symbol budget, improving reliability over both reactive and static baselines.
longDescription: This project models a 4-lane PAM4 SerDes link with DSP equalization (CTLE, FFE) and RS-FEC, then derives per-lane telemetry such as SQI, SNR, EVM, SER, and correction statistics to drive adaptive reliability control. A one-step-ahead lane-health predictor is trained on telemetry history and integrated into a constrained controller that reallocates parity across lanes under a fixed total symbol budget instead of increasing redundancy globally. The system is evaluated against both a reactive current-state allocator and a static equal-allocation baseline. In the strongest operating regime, the predictive controller achieved 0.0000 BLER at a fixed 64-symbol total parity budget, outperforming the reactive allocator (0.0013 BLER) and static equal allocation (0.0302 BLER) over the evaluation window. The framework also includes sweep analysis, controller timelines, parity heatmaps, and baseline comparisons to show how predictive control improves reliability without increasing total overhead.
highlights: Built a 4-lane PAM4 link simulator with DSP equalization, RS-FEC, and per-lane telemetry extraction for adaptive reliability control.
Extracted lane-health signals including SQI, SNR, EVM, SER, and correction statistics from the equalized link pipeline.
Trained a one-step-ahead lane-health predictor with ROC-AUC = 0.982 and integrated it into the controller loop for proactive parity allocation.
Designed a constrained cross-lane parity allocator that redistributes redundancy under a fixed total symbol budget rather than increasing total overhead.
At a fixed 64-symbol parity budget, achieved 0.0000 BLER with predictive control versus 0.0013 for a reactive current-state allocator and 0.0302 for static equal allocation.
Validated the engineering value with predictive vs reactive vs static baseline comparisons under the same total parity budget.
Generated controller timelines, parity heatmaps, BLER summaries, and sweep analyses to visualize cross-lane adaptation behavior.
Demonstrates the co-design of DSP telemetry, lightweight prediction, and fixed-budget reliability control for high-speed interconnects.

title: Latency-Aware Audio Quality Prediction and Adaptive Post-Processing Runtime
subtitle: C++ audio runtime • ONNX MOS prediction • Adaptive DSP • Real-time latency
description: Real-time C++ and ONNX Runtime audio pipeline that predicts frame-level perceptual quality and adaptively applies DSP post-processing while staying below a 10 ms hop-time budget.
longDescription: Built a latency-aware audio ML-to-runtime system that predicts MOS-like perceptual quality on short audio frames, selects bypass/light/strong post-processing modes, and measures per-frame latency across feature extraction, ONNX inference, DSP, and total runtime. The system uses a lightweight MLP MOS regressor exported to ONNX, C++ feature extraction, adaptive mode selection, overlap-add reconstruction, CSV diagnostics, batch evaluation, and generated plots for MOS timeline, mode timeline, latency distribution, stage latency, and before/after spectrogram comparisons.
metrics: MLP MOS regressor: MAE 0.3687, RMSE 0.4680, Pearson 0.7801
Runtime stayed below 10 ms hop-time budget across tested clips
Average frame time reduced from 5.2361 ms to 2.4691 ms after spectral front-end optimization
highlights: Built a real-time-style C++ audio runtime using 25 ms frames and 10 ms hop processing.
Trained a lightweight MLP regressor for continuous MOS-like perceptual quality prediction and exported it to ONNX for C++ inference.
Implemented adaptive mode selection: bypass, light post-processing, or stronger post-processing based on predicted frame quality.
Integrated deterministic DSP post-processing with smoothing, soft limiting, and overlap-add reconstruction.
Logged frame-level diagnostics including predicted MOS, selected mode, feature time, ONNX inference time, DSP time, and total frame time.
Generated runtime plots for predicted MOS timeline, mode timeline, latency timeline, latency histogram, stage latency, and before/after spectrograms.
Optimized the spectral front-end by precomputing DFT twiddle factors, reducing average total frame time from 5.2361 ms to 2.4691 ms.
Demonstrated latency-aware adaptive processing where low-MOS clips triggered more strong processing and higher-MOS clips triggered more bypass behavior.

title: FreeRTOS-Based STM32 Diagnostic Firmware Platform
subtitle: STM32 Cortex-M4 • UART shell • ADC/PWM/GPIO diagnostics • Fault self-test
description: Embedded C firmware for STM32 Nucleo-F446RE demonstrating a FreeRTOS diagnostic platform with UART commands, peripheral validation, fault injection, and manufacturing-style self-test routines.
longDescription: Built a FreeRTOS-based diagnostic firmware platform on STM32 Nucleo-F446RE to demonstrate microcontroller bring-up, RTOS task partitioning, UART diagnostics, peripheral validation, and fault-management workflows. The firmware exposes a serial command shell over the ST-LINK Virtual COM Port and supports system status reporting, RTOS task display, ADC internal-channel readout, PWM LED control, GPIO interrupt event tracking, fault injection/clearing, self-test execution, and software reset. The project is designed as an embedded firmware bring-up and diagnostics prototype similar to what is used during board validation and manufacturing test.
highlights: Built a FreeRTOS-based STM32 Cortex-M4 firmware platform with separate command, heartbeat, monitor, and fault-management tasks.
Implemented a UART diagnostic shell over ST-LINK Virtual COM Port at 115200 baud for status, version, RTOS task reporting, ADC readout, PWM control, fault handling, self-test, and software reset.
Validated board-level peripherals on STM32 Nucleo-F446RE including USART2, TIM2 PWM on PA5, ADC1 internal temperature/VREFINT channels, and GPIO EXTI interrupt on PC13.
Implemented fault injection and clearing logic where the fault manager overrides the normal heartbeat with a fast LED blink pattern.
Added manufacturing-style self-test routines that report PASS status for UART shell, FreeRTOS scheduler, PWM, GPIO interrupt path, ADC readout, and fault manager.
Used STM32CubeIDE, STM32 HAL, and PuTTY serial terminal to build, flash, debug, and verify the firmware.
